title: Inspect performance
body:
When running `skopeo inspect` the performance seems quite a bit worse than when using `docker`:

```
$ time skopeo inspect docker://mysql:latest > /dev/null
skopeo inspect docker://mysql:latest > /dev/null  0.09s user 0.03s system 2% cpu 4.266 total

$ time skopeo inspect docker-daemon:mysql:latest > /dev/null
skopeo inspect docker-daemon:mysql:latest > /dev/null  0.03s user 0.39s system 12% cpu 3.411 total

$ time docker inspect mysql:latest > /dev/null
docker inspect mysql:latest > /dev/null  0.02s user 0.02s system 121% cpu 0.028 total
```

Looking at some of the code, `NewImageSource()` for a docker daemon reference seems to take much longer than for a docker reference when I tried it out, though I'm not sure if this is the (only) issue.

comments.body:
Thanks for reaching out.

For `docker://`, you probably want to use `-n`.

`docker-daemon:` is, mostly, a one-time conversion mechanism. It is not intended to be a high-performance component of ongoing workflows, and it works by exporting the whole image from the daemon; that’s inherently expensive. If you have an image managed by a Docker daemon (and you are not planning to transition to some other location, like an image in Podman), using Docker’s native features directly is a very reasonable choice; the extra levels of abstraction implied by `skopeo` do have a cost.

Appreciate the quick response.

`-n` did help:

```
$ time skopeo inspect -n docker://mysql:latest > /dev/null
skopeo inspect -n docker://mysql:latest > /dev/null  0.05s user 0.04s system 3% cpu 2.784 total
```

It seems unfortunate that it needs to pull the whole image just to inspect it.
Just to be explicit, `inspect … docker://` _does not_ pull the whole image. (It does happen for `docker-daemon:`.)
A friendly reminder that this issue had no activity for 30 days.
Please reopen if there are outstanding questions.
